questions.scenario: A proposal reduces production app replicas from 3 to 1. It saves always-on compute cost, but the review has no traffic, deploy, restart, or failure evidence.

options.text: Pause the change until the team knows what availability promise the extra replicas support.
Approve it because every replica above 1 is always waste.
Approve it if the monthly bill is high enough, because cost evidence replaces health evidence.
Delete the database backups first, because compute replicas and backups protect the same failure.

questions.explanation: Reducing replicas can be safe, but the team needs evidence about traffic, deployments, health checks, and failure tolerance. Cost savings alone do not explain the resilience tradeoff.

questions.scenario: The nightly export runs once a day, can be rerun tomorrow, and is not on the checkout path. A teammate wants it to have the same warm standby design as checkout.

options.text: Ask whether the workflow promise justifies the steady cost, because not every workflow needs checkout-level readiness.
Require active-active design for every workflow so the architecture is symmetrical.
Remove all recovery protection because batch jobs never matter.
Move the export to a more expensive database tier because that always reduces RPO.

questions.explanation: Service promises should belong to workflows. A rerunnable daily export may accept longer delay than checkout, which lets the team spend differently without being careless.

questions.scenario: The team wants to disable blob versioning for receipt containers to reduce storage growth. Receipts are customer-visible, and the only current recovery path for overwrites is version restore.

options.text: Revise the plan unless another tested recovery path still protects receipt overwrites.
Approve it because current blobs remain visible, so older versions never matter.
Approve it if budgets are configured, because budget alerts restore deleted versions.
Replace versioning with more app instances, because compute replicas protect object history.

questions.explanation: Versioning may be the cost the team pays for recovering overwrites. If it is removed, the service promise needs a replacement or an explicit decision to accept more risk.

questions.scenario: Before approving a cost reduction, which review questions match the article?

options.text: Which cost shape changes, and which workflow owns that cost?
Which failure shape gets weaker if the resource or retention is reduced?
What signal proves the change is safe, and what is the rollback or recovery path?
Can the team skip owner review because Azure defaults are usually enough?

questions.explanation: The tradeoff table exists to keep cost, workflow, protection, evidence, and recovery in the same conversation. Defaults can help, but they do not replace a reviewed service promise.
